title: Observability Is A Missing Layer In AI-Era Chiplet Design
gist: An expert roundtable argues that in-silicon observability must become a first-class, fabric-aligned architectural layer for AI-era chiplet systems, with AI handling analysis (not collection or action), formal methods bounding AI controllers, and standardized secure telemetry being the key unlock for a true merchant-chiplet marketplace.

insights.claim: In chiplet architectures, observability must be designed as a fabric-aligned, cross-die telemetry plane so architects can correlate traffic, latency, congestion, and fault behavior across package boundaries without losing system context.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Frames on-die observability IP as a required architectural layer, not an add-on, expanding TAM for NoC/telemetry IP vendors like Arteris and Baya Systems.
insights.quote: observability must be designed as a fabric-aligned, cross-die telemetry plane so architects can correlate traffic, latency, congestion, and fault behavior across package boundaries
insights.locator: Key Takeaways

insights.claim: Scaling observability is fundamentally an architectural concern that AI cannot solve, because without consistent fabric-aligned visibility across dies, AI has little useful data to interpret.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Contrarian to AI-solves-everything framing: value accrues to instrumentation/architecture layer first; AI is downstream.
insights.quote: AI helps you understand the data, but scaling observability is really an architectural concern.
insights.locator: Nightingale: If we're talking about scaling observability

insights.claim: The observability challenge in modern chiplet systems has shifted from collecting data to interpreting it, as systems produce vast telemetry from numerous probes that can't be analyzed manually or with static rules.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Interpretation, not capture, is the bottleneck, favoring ML analytics and anomaly-detection layers over raw sensor count.
insights.quote: The main challenge now isn't collecting data, but interpreting it.
insights.locator: Nightingale: I agree. We're seeing agentic AI

insights.claim: On-die visibility must be extended to in-package visibility in the chiplet world, because components can look electrically healthy in isolation yet break in between at the bump or substrate.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Interconnect/substrate fault isolation is an underserved gap; watch tooling that localizes faults between dies as chiplets scale.
insights.quote: Things can look electrically healthy. They can look fine in isolation. But they break somewhere in between. Is it the bump? Is it the substrate?
insights.locator: Nayampally: I'm going to go off a slightly non-fabric end

themes.theme: AI division of labor: collect vs analyze vs act

insights.claim: Data collection in silicon remains deliberately conventional and deterministic because no one wants a 'smart' monitor that sometimes decides not to capture a critical error; AI's near-term value is concentrated in the analysis phase.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Sets realistic boundaries on AI-in-silicon hype: capture stays deterministic hardware/firmware; monetizable AI is analytics/anomaly detection.
insights.quote: Nobody wants a 'smart' monitor that sometimes decides not to capture a critical error.
insights.locator: Darbari: Collection remains largely conventional

insights.claim: The action side of silicon telemetry is still mostly rule-based and likely to remain so, with AI-assisted controllers over coming years bounded by clear safety envelopes and overseen by traditional control logic.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Tempers expectations for autonomous closed-loop AI control in silicon; incumbents' rule-based control logic retains relevance near-term.
insights.quote: In the near term, AI remains a powerful analyst and advisor, particularly in cloud and data center environments.
insights.locator: Darbari: Collection remains largely conventional

insights.claim: Small language models and TinyML are being deeply embedded in chips to analyze data on-chip in a tiny footprint, an area with little existing literature.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: On-chip TinyML for telemetry is an early, under-documented frontier; potential differentiation for IP and edge-analytics vendors.
insights.quote: They are analyzing data on chip in a tiny footprint, whether it's a hard implementation or whether it's soft.
insights.locator: Fish: The LLMs and AI applied across EDA

insights.claim: AI will eventually orchestrate data centers via swarms of agents making real-time decisions on telemetry too voluminous for any human to review, because the data volume behind these logs will be enormous.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Agentic orchestration of datacenter infrastructure is framed as the only viable path, supporting demand for autonomous ops tooling.
insights.quote: There's not going to be any human behind these logs, this telemetry, to make decisions because the data volume is enormous, and decisions will be made in real-time.
insights.locator: Merlo: In the future, if not already

insights.claim: By building AI reliability models around the many on-die monitor variants, it is becoming possible to predict almost down to the day when silicon in a data center will fail.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Day-level failure prediction enables proactive hardware swaps and life extension, a concrete value driver for SLM/monitor IP and hyperscaler uptime.
insights.quote: it's possible to start to predict in the context of a data center almost down to the day when the silicon is going to fail
insights.locator: Harrison: I've recently seen an example

insights.claim: Data center operators' core motivation for pervasive monitoring is to extend the useful life of data center hardware as much as possible.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Hardware-life extension is the demand driver; positions silicon lifecycle management as a capex-optimization sale to hyperscalers.
insights.quote: They want to extend the useful life of data center hardware as much as possible.
insights.locator: Harrison: I've recently seen an example

insights.claim: Self-healing silicon via core harvesting, swapping out cores as they fail, is emerging as a silicon-lifecycle-management capability.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Core-harvesting resilience could reshape reliability economics and warranty models for high-value AI silicon.
insights.quote: If you're able to do core harvesting and swap out when they fail, then you've got this nice terminology, which is, 'I've got a self-healing piece of silicon.'
insights.locator: Harrison: This is a silicon lifecycle management instrument

insights.claim: Bringing a training cluster down because one node is failing can cost hyperscalers thousands of dollars, so most operators will pay a premium for better telemetry to avoid catastrophic failures.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Quantifies the ROI case for telemetry spend: single-node failure cost justifies premium monitoring, expanding the addressable market.
insights.quote: Bringing a training cluster down because one node is failing can cost hyperscalers thousands of dollars.
insights.locator: Merlo: If you think about the cost associated with operating a data center

insights.claim: Robotics will be paired with predictive failure so that when a chip is predicted to go out of life in about a month, a robot can be staged to swap it out as soon as possible.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Links predictive telemetry to robotic datacenter maintenance, a second-order automation opportunity.
insights.locator: Merlo: If you throw robotics into the mix

insights.claim: Monitor and sensor data formats are 'all over the map' while hyperscalers want telemetry standardized up to Redfish, leaving an unresolved opportunity to standardize the actual data-sharing formats across multi-vendor IP in large systems.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Format fragmentation is a live standards gap; the winner of telemetry-schema standardization gains ecosystem leverage.
insights.quote: the monitor data and sensor data are all over the map. The hyperscalers want to see at least the telemetry standardized.
insights.locator: Fish: Standards have their advantages and disadvantages

insights.claim: The inability to isolate in-package faults (bump vs substrate) in real time is likely the single biggest barrier, apart from integration liability, preventing a true merchant chiplet marketplace.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Names the gating problem for the chiplet-marketplace thesis: solving fault attribution unlocks multi-vendor chiplet commerce.
insights.quote: That's probably the one thing that's preventing a true merchant chiplet marketplace, apart from the usual liability of who puts it together.
insights.locator: Nayampally: I'm going to go off a slightly non-fabric end

insights.claim: No single vendor can generate and process all the data needed for tomorrow's chips, so the industry must move toward an open telemetry framework enabling best-of-breed components and interoperability across management stacks.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: A vendor conceding the single-vendor model signals momentum toward open telemetry standards, favoring interoperable IP suppliers.
insights.quote: No single vendor can generate and process all the data needed for the chips of tomorrow.
insights.locator: Karvat: For us, it's not about additional capabilities

insights.claim: TSMC and other foundries are developing 3D chip solutions that require secure, encrypted communication between system components from different companies, since cracks or hotspots can propagate across chips via crosstalk.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Foundry-level 3D integration demands cross-company secure telemetry, an emerging requirement for chiplet security IP.
insights.locator: Radhakrishnan: TSMC and other foundries are developing 3D chip solutions

insights.claim: Formal methods can capture the invariants that AI-derived control policies must respect, providing a way to verify that no data-driven controller can drive a system into provably unsafe states.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Positions formal verification as the safety guardrail for AI control in silicon, a differentiated moat for formal-tools vendors.
insights.quote: formal properties can capture the invariants that AI-derived policies must respect
insights.locator: Darbari: Collection remains largely conventional

insights.claim: AI can flag that a telemetry pattern looks suspicious, but only formal methods can determine whether that pattern constitutes or could lead to a real property violation.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Delineates AI vs formal roles, arguing AI is not a substitute for structural verification, relevant to EDA tool positioning.
insights.quote: AI can tell you, 'This pattern looks suspicious.' But only formal can tell you whether that pattern constitutes, or could lead to, a real property violation.
insights.locator: Darbari: Scaling observability here is fundamentally an architecture and standards problem

insights.claim: Silent data corruption (SDC) is described as the problem nobody can afford to ignore anymore, requiring observability designed from the start with checkers and monitors instantiated from formal specifications to catch early symptoms.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: SDC is a rising reliability risk at scale, driving demand for formal-derived, property-aware telemetry and checkers.
insights.quote: Silent data corruption is the problem nobody can afford to ignore anymore.
insights.locator: Darbari: Three things stand out

themes.theme: Overhead, data movement, and telemetry security

insights.claim: Observability infrastructure is being built on a completely separate, passive network so it doesn't perturb the data being observed, invoking the Heisenberg uncertainty principle, with no cycles stolen from functional operation and impact limited to timing closure.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Out-of-band passive monitoring resolves the observer-effect objection, de-risking pervasive on-die instrumentation adoption.
insights.quote: It's the Heisenberg Uncertainty theorem, and we've got a different network to spy on or observe what's going on to mitigate that.
insights.locator: Nightingale: The impact is very low on our types of design

insights.claim: Monitor infrastructure has grown from single-temperature sensors to over 100 sense points or thousands of slack monitors on a chip, and like DFT before it, area impact no longer determines whether monitors get implemented.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Monitor density is now standard design practice, not an area-cost debate, signaling durable structural demand for monitor IP.
insights.quote: they go from being very trivial, single-temperature sensors, to literally over 100 sense points or thousands of slack monitors, and all on a chip
insights.locator: Fish: There is a broad spectrum

insights.claim: At scale, data movement is the limiting factor, so design teams must process, filter, and reduce raw telemetry near the sensor before it moves to an on-die service processor and off-die to the system stack or cloud.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Near-sensor data reduction is the scalability enabler, favoring architectures with hierarchical on-die processing.
insights.quote: At scale, it's the data movement that kills you.
insights.locator: Karvat: At scale, it's the data movement

insights.claim: Even seemingly innocuous monitor data such as temperature, voltage, and transactional data is treated as hypersensitive in automotive and data center contexts, making consistent protection across vendors a requirement.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Telemetry-data privacy is an unmet need, opening a security-schema opportunity for chiplet and monitor IP.
insights.quote: In automotive and in the data center, everybody's sensitive about this data.
insights.locator: Fish: I'm a little afraid of pointing the finger at myself

insights.claim: Telemetry is set to become a first-class citizen in complex systems, as important or more important than cooling, because the sheer scale of AI data center deployments makes it a must-have for hyperscalers.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Elevating telemetry to a datacenter design constraint on par with cooling signals sustained investment and priority spend.
insights.quote: Telemetry is going to become a first-class citizen in all these complex systems
insights.locator: Merlo: Telemetry is going to become a first-class citizen

insights.claim: A persistent gap in observability is that today's data tells you what happened but not why, and customers want traffic behavior correlated with workload, software state, and system configuration to make faults actionable.
insights.speaker: Ann Mutschler
insights.so_what: Root-cause context (the 'why') is the next competitive frontier in observability tooling and a stated customer demand.
insights.quote: We can see what happened, but we're not that much closer to working out how to fix it.
insights.locator: Nightingale: Today, a lot of observability tells you what happened
